title: Make the React checkout modal trap and restore focus for keyboard and screen-reader users
business_context: Our fintech checkout modal in Next.js loses focus to the page body when opened, so 4.1% of keyboard users abandon at the payment step (measured via session replay). Screen-reader users hear the background page read out behind the dialog. A WCAG 2.1 AA audit before a SOC2 renewal flagged 2.4.3 Focus Order and 1.3.1 Info and Relationships as blockers.
constraints: Must work across React 18 concurrent rendering and portal-based modals
No regression for existing 12 modal call sites
Focus restore must survive route changes triggered while modal is open
Bundle delta < 3KB gzip (focus-trap-react is 9KB and rejected)
Support nested modals (confirm-on-top-of-payment)

before_state.description: Modal renders into a portal with no focus management; Tab escapes to the page, and aria attributes are missing so VoiceOver reads the obscured page.

issues: No role=dialog / aria-modal so SR treats it as plain div
Tab moves focus to elements behind the overlay
Focus is never moved into the dialog on open nor restored on close
Background page is not inert, so SR reads obscured content

reasoning: Naive approach: add role="dialog" and call firstInput.focus() in a useEffect. Rejected pure-CSS pointer-events blocking because it does nothing for keyboard/SR users. Rejected focus-trap-react dependency for bundle budget. Chosen path traces UI->a11y tree: on open we capture document.activeElement, set aria-modal and inert on siblings (with focus-trap fallback via a Tab keydown handler that wraps between first/last tabbable), move focus to the first tabbable or the dialog itself; on close we restore focus to the saved element. A focus sentinel stack supports nested modals. No API/data layer changes; config exposes a `inertPolyfill` flag for Safari < 16.

after_state.description: Accessible dialog with focus trap, restore, inert background, and nested-modal stack.

files.purpose: Maintains a LIFO stack so nested modals restore focus to the correct prior element.
Computes visible, focusable elements for wrap-around trapping without a dependency.
Adds dialog semantics, focus trap with wrap-around, background inert, and restore on unmount.
Lazily polyfills inert for Safari < 16 so background blocking works there too.

migration_strategy: Ship focusStack/tabbable utils behind a `a11yModal` flag defaulting off
Enable flag for internal QA + axe-core CI gate on the checkout route
Canary to 5% of traffic, watch keyboard-abandonment metric and SR bounce
Ramp to 100% over 3 days; keep old codepath importable
Delete legacy Modal body and the flag after 2 weeks clean
testing_strategy: Jest + Testing Library fixture renders Modal open with three buttons; assert document.activeElement is the first button after open, Tab from last wraps to first, Shift+Tab from first wraps to last, and after close activeElement equals the trigger that opened it. Side-effect assert: sibling nodes gain/lose the `inert` attribute. axe-core run asserts zero violations. Chaos test: unmount the trigger element while modal is open, then close, and assert focus falls back to document.body without throwing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive role+focus() approach lets Tab escape the dialog (no wrap), never restores focus on close, and leaves the background readable by VoiceOver because nothing is inert; nested modals also stomp each other's restore target.

title: Replace hardcoded English currency/date strings with ICU MessageFormat across the Next.js billing UI
business_context: A B2B SaaS billing dashboard hardcodes '$%s' and 'MM/DD/YYYY' everywhere, so EU customers (38% of ARR) see USD symbols on EUR invoices and ambiguous dates that triggered 1,900 support tickets last quarter. Finance flagged a near-miss where a German customer read 03/04 as April 3 instead of March 4.
constraints: Must support en-US, de-DE, fr-FR, ja-JP at launch with pluralization
Server and client must format identically (no hydration mismatch)
Currency must respect each invoice's stored currency code, not the UI locale
No blocking on a translation vendor; keys ship with English fallback
Number/date formatting must use Intl, not a 70KB moment-locale bundle

before_state.description: Strings and formatting are concatenated by hand; currency symbol is fixed to '$' and dates use a custom formatter.

issues: Currency symbol hardcoded to '$' regardless of inv.currency
Date format is US-only and ambiguous for non-US locales
No pluralization or translation of the word 'Due'
getMonth/getDate use local TZ, drifting the displayed day near midnight UTC

reasoning: Naive approach: sprinkle ternaries on navigator.language. Rejected react-intl's runtime-only setup because SSR would mismatch on hydration; rejected moment+locales for the 70KB cost. Chosen path: a thin Intl wrapper plus an ICU message catalog. Cross-layer trace: data carries the invoice currency; API stays unchanged; UI reads locale from an SSR-provided context so server and client share the same Intl.NumberFormat/DateTimeFormat; messages resolve from a per-locale JSON with English fallback; config sets supported locales; tests assert formatting per locale; rollout gates behind a `intlBilling` flag.

after_state.description: Locale context + Intl-based formatters + ICU catalog; currency from invoice, locale from request.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for currency/date formatting; currency arg decoupled from UI locale; UTC default avoids day drift.
ICU catalog with plural rules; English is the canonical fallback.
German overrides; missing keys fall through to en.json at load time.
Provides locale+messages from SSR so server and client render identically; useT() formats ICU.
Renders currency from the invoice and a localized, unambiguous date via the shared formatters.

migration_strategy: Land format.ts + catalogs with en-only data behind `intlBilling` flag off
Wire I18nProvider in the SSR shell, passing request locale; default still en-US
Enable de/fr/ja catalogs for internal users, run hydration-mismatch CI check
Canary by locale: route 10% of de-DE sessions through the new path
Ramp to all locales; keep legacy formatter exported for one release
Remove hardcoded formatter and the flag after dashboards confirm zero hydration warnings
testing_strategy: Snapshot fixture with one invoice in EUR and dueDate 2026-03-04T23:30:00Z. Assert money() renders '4,99\u00a0\u20ac' for de-DE and '\u20ac4.99' for en-US (output assertion), and dateMedium renders '4. März 2026' for de-DE and stays March 4 (not March 5) confirming the UTC side of the TZ fix. Negative test: pass currency 'XYZ' and assert it throws a RangeError that the row boundary catches and renders the raw amount. Hydration test: render server HTML and client HTML for de-DE and assert byte-equality.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-concatenated '$' shows USD on EUR invoices, US date order misreads as the wrong day for de-DE, and a navigator.language client-only approach desyncs from the server-rendered HTML causing React hydration mismatches.

title: Add RTL mirroring to the Vue/Nuxt dashboard layout for Arabic and Hebrew without forking components
business_context: An e-commerce platform expanding to Saudi Arabia and Israel renders the entire Nuxt dashboard left-to-right, so Arabic users see truncated, reversed UI: the sidebar sits on the wrong side and chevrons point the wrong way. A pre-launch review with 12 native testers rated navigation 'unusable' (2.1/5).
constraints: Must flip layout direction per locale without duplicating 140 components
Logical CSS only; no hardcoded left/right that would need manual mirroring
Icons that imply direction (back, send, progress) must flip; brand logos must NOT
Direction must be set before first paint to avoid an LTR->RTL flash
Third-party charts that don't support RTL must stay LTR inside an RTL page

before_state.description: Hardcoded margin-left/padding-right and dir is never set; chevrons and the drawer are physically positioned.

issues: left:0 and padding-left pin the nav to the physical left in RTL
chevron-right always points right even in RTL
text-align:left ignores direction
No dir attribute set on html, so the browser never mirrors

reasoning: Naive approach: write a second RTL stylesheet with mirrored rules. Rejected because 140 components would each need a parallel sheet and would drift. Rejected runtime DOM-mirroring libraries (rtlcss at runtime) for the FOUC and chart breakage. Chosen path: convert physical properties to CSS logical properties (margin-inline-start, inset-inline-start, text-align:start), set dir via Nuxt server middleware before paint, flip directional icons via a CSS [dir=rtl] transform scoped to a class, and wrap non-RTL charts in a forced dir=ltr island. Trace: locale->server middleware sets <html dir>; CSS logical props mirror automatically; icon component opts into flipping; config maps locale->dir; tests assert computed inset.

after_state.description: Logical-property layout, server-set dir, opt-in icon flipping, and LTR islands for charts.

files.purpose: Resolves direction on the server so <html dir> is correct in the first byte, avoiding a flash.

files.content:
<template><NuxtPage /></template>
<script setup>
const dir = useRequestEvent()?.context.dir ?? 'ltr';
useHead({ htmlAttrs: { dir, lang: useCookie('locale').value ?? 'en' } });
</script>
files.purpose: Applies the resolved dir/lang to the html element on SSR.
Uses logical properties so the same CSS mirrors in RTL; chevron flips only when dir=rtl.
Forces LTR around third-party charts that cannot render RTL, isolating them from the page direction.

migration_strategy: Codemod physical CSS props to logical props behind a `rtl` flag; visual diff in LTR shows no change
Ship server middleware computing dir; keep default ltr for all locales
Enable ar/he for internal testers, run a Playwright RTL screenshot suite
Canary RTL to 10% of ar-SA traffic; monitor nav-tap heatmaps
Ramp to 100% for RTL locales; wrap remaining charts in LtrIsland as found
Remove the flag once visual-regression baselines are green for two weeks
testing_strategy: Playwright fixture loads the dashboard with locale=ar; assert getComputedStyle(nav).insetInlineStart resolves to the right edge (document.dir==='rtl'), the chevron has matrix(-1,...) transform, and the chart island reports direction:ltr. Output assert: text-align computed value is 'right' for RTL, 'left' for LTR. Side-effect: the html element carries dir='rtl' in the initial SSR response (assert on raw HTML, proving no FOUC). Chaos test: switch locale cookie mid-session and assert the next SSR navigation flips dir without a client-only flash.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A parallel RTL stylesheet drifts from the LTR one and still mis-mirrors any component using physical left/right; a runtime mirroring lib flashes LTR-then-RTL on first paint and corrupts charts that assume LTR coordinates.

title: Give the Angular data grid accessible sortable column headers with aria-sort and keyboard activation
business_context: A logistics B2B grid (Angular) shows shipment tables where sorting is triggered by a click handler on a plain <div>. Screen-reader users can't tell a column is sortable or which direction is active, and keyboard users can't sort at all. An accessibility complaint from a partner's compliance team threatened to pause a 7-figure renewal.
constraints: Headers must be operable by mouse, Enter, and Space
aria-sort must reflect none/ascending/descending and update live
Must not break the existing 9 grids that bind to the same component
Sorting 50k client-side rows must stay under 16ms to avoid jank
Keyboard focus order must follow visual column order including hidden columns

before_state.description: Header cells are clickable divs with no role, no tabindex, and no aria-sort.

files.content: {{ c.label }} {{ sortDir === 'asc' ? '\u25b2' : '\u25bc' }}
issues: Header is a div, not a th/button, so SR announces nothing actionable
No tabindex so keyboard cannot reach it
Sort direction conveyed only by a visual arrow glyph
No aria-sort, so direction isn't in the accessibility tree

reasoning: Naive approach: add (keydown.enter) to the div. Rejected because a div still isn't announced as a control and Space won't scroll-suppress correctly. Rejected swapping to native <table> wholesale for risk to 9 dependent grids. Chosen path: render semantic role=columnheader with aria-sort, embed a real <button> for activation (free keyboard + SR semantics), keep the existing sort() logic. Trace: UI uses button+aria-sort; component logic unchanged; sort comparator memoized to hold 16ms; config unaffected; tests assert aria-sort transitions; rollout per-grid via input flag.

after_state.description: Semantic sortable headers with aria-sort, button activation, and memoized comparison.

files.purpose: Uses columnheader role + aria-sort and a real button so SR/keyboard work; arrow is decorative.
Computes aria-sort and a descriptive label announcing the next action; preserves prior sort semantics.
Stable, allocation-free comparator factory so sorting 50k rows stays within frame budget.

migration_strategy: Add the semantic header template behind a `semanticHeaders` @Input defaulting false
Flip the flag on one internal grid; run axe-core and manual NVDA pass
Enable for 3 low-traffic grids; watch sort latency telemetry stays <16ms
Roll out to all 9 grids; keep the old template branch for one sprint
Remove the legacy div header and the flag after green a11y CI
testing_strategy: Karma/Jasmine fixture renders header with 3 columns; click a header and assert the columnheader's aria-sort becomes 'ascending', click again -> 'descending', click a different column -> previous becomes 'none'. Assert the button's aria-label text updates to announce the next direction. Performance side-effect test: sort a 50k-row fixture and assert the comparator runs under a 16ms budget via performance.now. Negative test: focus the header with Tab and dispatch a Space keydown, asserting sort fires (button semantics) and the page does not scroll.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding keydown to a div still leaves SR users without a 'sortable button' announcement and without aria-sort state; Space activation silently scrolls instead of sorting because a div has no implicit button behavior.

title: Make toast notifications announce to screen readers via an aria-live region in the React design system
business_context: A healthcare portal's React toast system mounts and unmounts toasts with no live region, so screen-reader patients never hear 'Prescription submitted' or critical 'Session expiring' warnings. A clinician using JAWS missed a failed-save toast and lost charting notes; the incident was logged as a patient-safety near-miss.
constraints: Polite vs assertive must be chosen per toast (errors assertive, info polite)
Rapid bursts of toasts must not clobber each other in the live region
Live region must exist before the first announcement (SR caches region at mount)
Must dedupe identical messages fired twice within 500ms
Works with React 18 StrictMode double-invoke in dev

before_state.description: Toasts render visually but there is no aria-live region; SR users get nothing.

issues: No aria-live region, so SR announces nothing
Errors and info use the same channel/politeness
Toasts removed from DOM before SR can read them
No dedupe; a double-fired error reads twice

reasoning: Naive approach: slap aria-live="polite" on the existing toast-host. Rejected because mixing polite info and assertive errors in one region loses urgency and removing nodes mid-read cuts off JAWS. Rejected react-aria's full announcer dep to keep the DS dependency-free. Chosen path: two persistent visually-hidden live regions (polite + assertive) mounted once at app root; announcements push text into the matching region and stay long enough to be read; a dedupe map suppresses repeats. Trace: store dispatches toast -> announcer routes by kind -> region text updates -> visual toast independent -> config tunes timing -> tests assert region content.

after_state.description: Dual persistent live regions with politeness routing, dedupe, and read-stable content.

files.purpose: Two always-mounted regions so SR caches them; errors use assertive+role=alert, info uses polite.
Routes by politeness, dedupes within 500ms, and clears+sets so identical consecutive messages still re-announce.
Decouples visual toasts (aria-hidden) from announcements; StrictMode double-invoke guarded by the seen ref.

migration_strategy: Mount LiveRegions at app root behind `liveToasts` flag, regions empty by default
Enable for internal QA; verify with NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver across info/error
Canary 10%; monitor that error toasts interrupt and info toasts queue politely
Ramp to 100%; keep visual toast path unchanged the whole time
Remove flag after SR smoke tests pass on three engines
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders LiveRegions + ToastHost; dispatch an info toast and assert the polite region's textContent equals the message after the 50ms tick, and the assertive region stays empty. Dispatch an error and assert role=alert region updates. Dedupe test: fire the same error twice within 200ms and assert the region text was set only once (spy on the store). Chaos test: under React StrictMode double-mount, assert each toast announces exactly once (seen ref + dedupe), not twice.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Putting aria-live on the toast-host announces info and error at the same politeness and gets cut off because the toast node is removed mid-read; double-fired errors read twice and StrictMode causes a duplicate announcement.

title: Localize FastAPI validation error messages with per-request locale negotiation and ICU plurals
business_context: A fintech API (Python/FastAPI) returns English-only Pydantic validation errors, so mobile clients in Brazil and Japan display raw 'field required' strings to end users. Localization was done client-side by string-matching English messages, which broke silently when FastAPI upgraded and reworded errors, causing 6 days of garbled forms in pt-BR.
constraints: Locale from Accept-Language with a supported-locale fallback chain
Messages must be data-driven (no English string matching by clients)
Pluralization for 'N characters' style messages via ICU
Must not leak field internals; map Pydantic error types to safe keys
Stateless across 12 Gunicorn workers; no per-process locale globals

before_state.description: Custom exception handler returns raw Pydantic errors in English regardless of Accept-Language.

files.content:
from fastapi import Request
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse

async def validation_handler(request: Request, exc: RequestValidationError):
    return JSONResponse(status_code=422, content={
        'errors': [{'loc': e['loc'], 'msg': e['msg'], 'type': e['type']} for e in exc.errors()]
    })
issues: msg is the raw English Pydantic string
Clients string-match 'field required' to translate, breaking on upgrades
loc leaks internal field paths
No Accept-Language handling at all

reasoning: Naive approach: keep returning English and let clients translate. Rejected because string-matching English is brittle and broke on a FastAPI bump. Rejected a global babel singleton because it is not safe across Gunicorn workers and request-scoped locale. Chosen path: map Pydantic error 'type' to stable message keys, negotiate locale per request, render with an ICU formatter, and return both a stable code and a localized message. Trace: middleware parses Accept-Language -> handler maps type->key -> ICU catalog renders with ctx (limit, etc.) -> response carries code+message -> config lists locales -> tests assert pt-BR/ja output.

after_state.description: Type->key mapping, per-request locale negotiation, ICU rendering, and a stable machine code.

files.purpose: Negotiates a supported locale per request and maps Pydantic types to stable codes+message keys.
Portuguese catalog with ICU plural for character counts.
Japanese catalog; Japanese has no plural distinction so one form suffices.
Negotiates locale, maps type->code/key, renders ICU per error, returns stable code + localized message without leaking full loc path.

migration_strategy: Add codes+message keys alongside the existing 'msg' field (additive, backward-compatible)
Ship pt_BR/ja catalogs; default locale negotiation to 'en'
Have one mobile client read 'code' instead of string-matching 'msg'
Canary Accept-Language negotiation; verify pt-BR and ja render correctly
Deprecate the raw 'msg' field after all clients consume 'code'
Remove client-side English string matching once telemetry shows zero usage
testing_strategy: Pytest fixture posts an invalid body (missing required field, too-short string) with Accept-Language 'pt-BR,pt;q=0.9'. Assert response 'errors'[0].code=='field_required' and message=='nome é obrigatório', and the too-short error renders '...ao menos 3 caracteres' (ICU plural other-form). Side-effect assert: _catalog lru_cache hit count increments only once per locale across requests (no per-request file read). Negative test: Accept-Language 'xx' falls back to 'en' and never raises. Concurrency test: fire 200 concurrent requests with mixed locales and assert no cross-locale message bleed (stateless).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning English and string-matching it client-side breaks the instant FastAPI/Pydantic reword the message; a global babel singleton races across Gunicorn workers and returns the wrong request's locale under load.

title: Add accessible names and roles to the icon-only toolbar buttons in the React editor
business_context: A devtools code editor's toolbar is a row of icon-only buttons (bold, link, undo) with no text. Screen-reader users hear 'button' twelve times with no way to tell them apart, and the WCAG 4.1.2 Name/Role/Value check failed in an automated CI gate that now blocks merges.
constraints: Every button needs a programmatic name without showing visible text
Toggle buttons (bold/italic) must expose aria-pressed state
Tooltips must be reachable by keyboard, not hover-only
Must not change the visual toolbar at all
Names must be translatable, not hardcoded English in JSX

before_state.description: Icon buttons render an SVG with no label, title, or aria attributes.

issues: No accessible name; SR reads 'button' only
Toggle state (bold on/off) not exposed via aria-pressed
No keyboard-reachable tooltip
Command labels are not translatable

reasoning: Naive approach: add a title attribute to each button. Rejected because title tooltips are hover-only (no keyboard access) and SR support is inconsistent. Rejected visible text labels (breaks the compact toolbar design). Chosen path: aria-label from a translated map for the name, aria-pressed for toggles, and a real tooltip component that opens on focus and hover with aria-describedby. Trace: command id -> t(label key) -> aria-label + tooltip -> aria-pressed from active set -> config holds label keys -> tests assert names/pressed.

after_state.description: Translated aria-labels, aria-pressed toggles, and focus-reachable tooltips.

files.purpose: Declares which commands are toggles and maps each to a translatable label key.
Tooltip that opens on focus and hover and is linked via aria-describedby for keyboard + SR users.
Gives each button a translated accessible name, exposes aria-pressed for toggles, and wires a keyboard-reachable tooltip; SVG marked decorative.

migration_strategy: Add role=toolbar and aria-labels behind a `toolbarA11y` flag; visuals unchanged
Enable flag in CI and run the axe 4.1.2 gate on the editor route
Dogfood with internal SR users to validate names and toggle announcements
Roll out to all users; keep old toolbar render branch one release
Remove the legacy branch and flag after axe passes for two weeks
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders Toolbar with active={new Set(['bold'])}; assert getByRole('button', { name: 'Bold' }) has aria-pressed='true' and the italic button has aria-pressed='false'. Tooltip test: focus the link button and assert a role=tooltip with the translated label becomes visible and the button's aria-describedby points to it. i18n side-effect: switch the provider to de and assert names become 'Fett'/'Kursiv'. Negative test: assert undo (non-toggle) has no aria-pressed attribute (undefined), not aria-pressed='false'.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A title attribute is hover-only so keyboard users still get no tooltip and SR support is flaky; without aria-pressed, toggle buttons never announce their on/off state, failing 4.1.2.

title: Implement locale-aware pluralization and gender in Django notification templates
business_context: A social platform (Django) builds notification strings with f-strings like f'{n} likes', which is wrong in Russian (3 plural forms), Arabic (6), and Polish, and ignores gender for 'X started following you'. Localization QA found 40% of pt/ru/ar notifications grammatically broken, hurting trust scores in those markets.
constraints: Must support en, ru, ar, pl plural rules and gender select
Notification fan-out runs in Celery workers; rendering must be thread-safe
Translators edit .po files, not code; keys must be stable
Render at send-time using the recipient's locale, not the actor's
Throughput: 8k notifications/sec; per-message render budget < 0.5ms

before_state.description: f-string templates assume English plural/gender and break for inflected languages.

issues: English-only plural: 'others' wrong for ru/ar/pl
Gender ignored; ru/pl verbs inflect by actor gender
No locale parameter; always English
Recipient locale never consulted

reasoning: Naive approach: add if n==1 branches in Python. Rejected because Russian/Arabic need 3-6 categories, not two, and gender adds another axis Python ifs can't scale. Rejected gettext ngettext alone because it covers plural but not gendered select. Chosen path: ICU MessageFormat catalogs per locale with plural + select, rendered against the recipient's locale at send time, cached compiled. Trace: Celery task loads recipient.locale -> selects catalog -> ICU renders with {count, gender} -> output string -> .po-like JSON catalogs editable by translators -> tests assert ru/ar forms.

after_state.description: ICU plural+select catalogs rendered per recipient locale, compiled+cached, thread-safe.

files.purpose: Russian catalog using ICU plural categories (one/few/many) and gendered verb select.
English fallback with correct one/other plural and no gender inflection.
Compiles ICU messages once (lru_cache) under a lock for thread-safe Celery use; renders by recipient locale.
Renders each notification in the recipient's locale at send time inside the fan-out task.

migration_strategy: Add ICU catalogs with en parity; render path behind a `icuNotify` setting
Enable for ru/ar/pl recipients only; English path unchanged
Have translators populate few/many forms via the JSON catalogs
Canary 5% of ru notifications; compare grammar-QA scores
Ramp to all inflected locales; keep f-string path importable
Delete f-string renderers and the setting after QA scores recover
testing_strategy: Pytest fixture renders 'like' for ru with count in {1, 3, 5}; assert one/few/many forms ('1 человек', '3 человека', '5 человек') exactly. Render 'follow' for ru with gender female and assert 'начала', male -> 'начал'. Side-effect: assert _compiled.cache_info().misses == number of distinct (locale,key) pairs across a 1000-message batch (compile-once). Performance test: render 8000 messages and assert mean per-message render < 0.5ms. Negative/chaos: missing 'tr' catalog falls back to en without raising, asserted under concurrent rendering from 16 threads with no cross-locale corruption.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Python if n==1 branches only model two plural categories, so Russian/Arabic/Polish render grammatically wrong forms (e.g. '3 человек' instead of 'человека'), and gender-blind templates inflect verbs incorrectly.

title: Build an accessible combobox with ARIA 1.2 patterns to replace a div-based autocomplete in React
business_context: A B2B SaaS app's customer-search autocomplete is a styled div listening to input events; screen-reader users get no announcement of results, no active-option indication, and arrow keys do nothing. The component is used in 30 places and failed the WAI-ARIA combobox conformance test during an enterprise procurement security/a11y review.
constraints: Follow the ARIA 1.2 combobox pattern (aria-expanded, aria-activedescendant, listbox)
Arrow Up/Down, Home/End, Enter, Escape must all work
Async results: announce result count politely without spamming
Must keep the existing onSelect(item) API so 30 call sites don't change
Virtualized list of up to 5k options must keep aria-activedescendant correct

before_state.description: A div with an input and a result div; no roles, no keyboard, no active-option tracking.

issues: No role=combobox/listbox/option; SR announces nothing
No keyboard navigation of results
No aria-activedescendant for the focused option
Result count never announced

reasoning: Naive approach: add onKeyDown to move a CSS .active class. Rejected because moving DOM focus into the list breaks the typing input and a CSS class isn't in the a11y tree. Rejected a headless lib swap given the 30 call sites and custom styling. Chosen path: implement the ARIA 1.2 combobox with aria-activedescendant (focus stays in input), role=listbox/option, full keyboard map, and a polite live count, preserving onSelect. Trace: input owns focus -> aria-activedescendant points at the highlighted option id -> arrow keys move index -> Enter calls onSelect -> live region announces 'N results' -> tests assert roles+keys -> rollout per call site via prop.

after_state.description: ARIA 1.2 combobox: input keeps focus, aria-activedescendant tracks option, full keyboard, polite count.

files.purpose: Encapsulates the ARIA combobox keyboard model and active-index state without moving DOM focus.
Full ARIA 1.2 combobox: roles, aria-expanded/controls/activedescendant, polite count, unchanged onSelect.
Windowing helper that always includes the active index so aria-activedescendant never points at an unmounted node.

migration_strategy: Ship the ARIA combobox as a new export; old div component stays for compatibility
Migrate 3 low-risk call sites; run the WAI-ARIA combobox conformance suite
Add an axe-core gate that fails on missing combobox roles
Migrate remaining 27 call sites in batches behind per-site adoption
Remove the legacy Autocomplete export after all sites migrate
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture mounts the combobox, types 'ac', and resolves 3 items; assert role=combobox input gains aria-expanded='true' and the listbox has 3 options. Keyboard test: ArrowDown sets aria-activedescendant to opt-0, again -> opt-1, End -> opt-2, Enter calls onSelect with items[2]; Escape sets aria-expanded='false'. Live-region side-effect: assert the polite region reads '3 results'. Virtualization test: with 5000 options, press End and assert the active option (id opt-4999) is mounted so aria-activedescendant resolves to a real node. Negative test: Enter with active=-1 does not call onSelect.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Moving a CSS .active class via onKeyDown puts nothing in the accessibility tree, so SR users hear no active option; and virtualization that unmounts the active option leaves aria-activedescendant pointing at a missing id, breaking SR focus entirely.

title: Fix non-localized number and unit formatting in the Go IoT telemetry dashboard API
business_context: A Go service powering an IoT fleet dashboard formats sensor values with fmt.Sprintf('%.2f'), emitting '1234.50' and '°F' to every client. EU operators expect '1.234,50' and Celsius; a German plant operator misread a temperature threshold, triggering a false shutdown that cost an estimated 4 hours of downtime.
constraints: Locale-aware decimal/grouping separators (de-DE uses '.' grouping, ',' decimal)
Unit system per operator (metric vs imperial) with correct conversion
Stateless across horizontally-scaled pods; locale from request, not env
Backward-compatible JSON shape; add fields, don't rename
Sub-millisecond formatting; called 200k times/sec at peak

before_state.description: Numbers and units are hardcoded US-style and Fahrenheit regardless of operator locale.

<template>

    <option v-for="l in SUPPORTED" :key="l" :value="l">{{ names[l] }}</option>

<script setup lang="ts">

import { SUPPORTED } from '../i18n';
import { loadLocale } from '../i18n/loader';

title: Make the Kafka-driven notification pipeline render emails in the recipient's locale and timezone
business_context: A logistics SaaS sends shipment-update emails via a Kafka consumer that formats timestamps in UTC and copy in English. constraints: Render at consume-time using recipient locale + IANA timezone from profile

No recipient locale/timezone lookup

reasoning: Naive approach: convert to recipient TZ inline and English-only copy. Rejected because copy must localize and inline conversion duplicates logic. Stamp templateVersion on new events; default 'v1' for legacy
Enable idempotency keys first (safe even with old rendering)
Canary ja-JP recipients; verify Tokyo-time ETA and Japanese copy
Ramp to all locales; keep en/UTC fallback for missing profiles

title: Make the Rails server set Content-Language and negotiate locale from URL, cookie, and Accept-Language
business_context: A healthcare patient portal (Rails) always serves English because locale is read from a hardcoded I18n.default_locale. Spanish-speaking patients (29% of the panel) can't switch to Spanish, and SEO suffers because pages don't emit Content-Language or hreflang. A health-equity review flagged English-only as an access barrier.
constraints: Locale resolution order: URL param > cookie > Accept-Language > default
Must persist chosen locale in a cookie for return visits
Emit Content-Language header and html lang per request
No locale leakage between requests in the threaded Puma server
Only allow whitelisted locales; reject/strip injection in the param

before_state.description: Locale is the global default; no negotiation, no header, no persistence.

files.content:
class ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
  before_action :set_locale

  def set_locale
    I18n.locale = I18n.default_locale
  end
end
issues: Always uses default_locale; no negotiation
No Content-Language header emitted
Chosen locale not persisted across requests

reasoning: Naive approach: read params[:locale] and set I18n.locale. Rejected because I18n.locale is thread-global and leaks if not reset per request, and an unvalidated param allows injection. Rejected URL-only locale (loses cookie persistence). Chosen path: resolve locale via an ordered chain (param > cookie > Accept-Language > default) restricted to a whitelist, set it within I18n.with_locale around the action, persist to a cookie, and emit Content-Language. Trace: request -> ordered resolver -> whitelist guard -> with_locale block -> cookie persist -> Content-Language + html lang -> tests assert ordering + no leak.

after_state.description: Ordered, whitelisted locale resolution within with_locale, cookie persistence, and Content-Language header.

files.content:
module LocaleNegotiation
  extend ActiveSupport::Concern
  AVAILABLE = %i[en es fr pt].freeze

  included do
    around_action :switch_locale
  end

  private

  def switch_locale(&action)
    locale = resolve_locale
    I18n.with_locale(locale) do
      cookies.permanent[:locale] = locale
      response.headers['Content-Language'] = locale.to_s
      action.call
    end
  end

  def resolve_locale
    from_param || from_cookie || from_header || I18n.default_locale
  end

  def from_param
    whitelist(params[:locale])
  end

  def from_cookie
    whitelist(cookies[:locale])
  end

  def from_header
    parsed = request.env['HTTP_ACCEPT_LANGUAGE'].to_s.scan(/[a-z]{2}/i).map(&:downcase)
    parsed.map { |l| whitelist(l) }.compact.first
  end

  def whitelist(val)
    sym = val.to_s.downcase.to_sym
    AVAILABLE.include?(sym) ? sym : nil
  end
end
files.purpose: Resolves locale by URL>cookie>Accept-Language>default, validates against a whitelist, scopes I18n with with_locale to avoid thread leakage, persists cookie, and sets Content-Language.

files.content:
class ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
  include LocaleNegotiation
end

files.content:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="<%= I18n.locale %>">
  <head>
    <% AVAILABLE_LOCALES.each do |loc| %>
      <link rel="alternate" hreflang="<%= loc %>" href="<%= url_for(locale: loc) %>">
    <% end %>
  </head>
  <body><%= yield %></body>
</html>

Ordering test: with cookie=fr and Accept-Language 'pt', GET /?locale=es resolves es (param wins); without param, cookie fr wins; without both, header pt wins. Security/negative test: GET /?locale=../../etc resolves to default (whitelist strips it) and never sets a bad cookie. Concurrency/chaos: fire interleaved es and fr requests on a threaded Puma and assert no request renders the other's locale (with_locale isolation).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Setting the thread-global I18n.locale without with_locale leaks the previous request's locale to the next thread-pooled request under Puma, and an unvalidated params[:locale] lets an attacker inject an arbitrary value into the cookie and Content-Language header.

Failed transfers from mis-parsed amounts generated 430 support tickets and one duplicate payment.
constraints: Mask grouping/decimal separators must match the locale
Indian numbering (lakh/crore) grouping for en-IN
Parse back to integer minor units exactly (no float drift)
Currency symbol/position from locale, not hardcoded prefix
Keep the field controlled and accessible (inputmode, aria)

before_state.description: A fixed US mask and '$' prefix corrupt non-US input and use float math.

issues: Strips ',' so de-DE '1.000,50' becomes 100050
Hardcoded '$' prefix and US grouping
parseFloat introduces float drift; emits dollars not minor units
No inputmode/aria for numeric entry

reasoning: Naive approach: regex per locale. Rejected because hand-rolled separator regex won't handle Indian lakh grouping or symbol position. Rejected emitting float dollars (cent drift). Chosen path: derive separators from Intl.NumberFormat.formatToParts, parse the user's locale-formatted string to integer minor units, format display using the locale, and emit minor units. Trace: locale -> Intl parts give group/decimal symbols -> parse to minor units -> controlled display formatted by locale -> emit integer cents -> inputmode/aria -> tests assert de-DE/en-IN round-trips.

after_state.description: Intl-derived separators, locale-aware grouping (incl. Indian), integer minor units, accessible input.

files.purpose: Derives locale separators (handles Indian grouping via Intl), parses to exact integer minor units, and formats display.
Accepts locale separators, emits exact minor units, reformats on blur, and exposes inputmode/aria-label.

migration_strategy: Ship localeNumber utils + new AmountInput behind `localeAmount` flag
Default to en-US formatting so US users are unaffected
Canary de-DE and en-IN; verify round-trip parse to minor units
Add a server-side cross-check that the submitted minor units match displayed text
Ramp to all locales; remove the hardcoded '$' field
Delete the legacy float path after zero mis-parse tickets for 2 weeks
testing_strategy: Vitest fixture: parseToMinor('1.000,50','de-DE') === 100050; parseToMinor('1,00,000.00','en-IN') === 10000000; parseToMinor('','en-US') === null. formatMinor(100050,'de-DE','EUR') groups as '1.000,50\u00a0\u20ac'. Component test: type '1.000,50' under de-DE and assert onChange last received 100050 (integer, no float). Blur side-effect: assert the field reformats to grouped display. Negative test: typing '12.34.56' (malformed) yields onChange(null) so the form blocks submit. Float-drift test: parseToMinor('0.07','en-US')===7 and round-trips without 0.06999... error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A locale-specific regex still can't express Indian lakh grouping and parseFloat on de-DE strings drops the comma decimal, turning '1.000,50' into 100050 and introducing cent drift that caused duplicate/incorrect transfers.

title: Add reduced-motion support and accessible animations to the Next.js onboarding flow
business_context: A B2B SaaS onboarding uses large parallax and auto-playing motion. Users with vestibular disorders reported nausea, and one filed an ADA complaint. The animations also ignore prefers-reduced-motion, and an auto-advancing carousel gives keyboard/SR users no control, failing WCAG 2.2.2 Pause/Stop/Hide and 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions.
constraints: Respect prefers-reduced-motion at the OS level and a user toggle
Auto-advancing carousel must be pausable and not steal focus
Reduced-motion must still convey state (cross-fade, not jump)
SSR-safe: no hydration mismatch from media-query reads
Animations gated server-side so no motion flashes before JS loads

before_state.description: Parallax + auto-advance run unconditionally; no reduced-motion handling, no pause control.

issues: Parallax/transition always on; ignores prefers-reduced-motion
Carousel auto-advances with no pause control
Auto-advance can move focus/content out from under SR users
No CSS gate, so motion flashes before JS

reasoning: Naive approach: read window.matchMedia('prefers-reduced-motion') in an effect. Rejected because that's client-only and causes an SSR hydration mismatch and a motion flash. Rejected disabling all animation (loses helpful state cues). Chosen path: gate motion via CSS @media (prefers-reduced-motion) plus a user toggle persisted in a cookie read on the server, pause auto-advance on reduced motion or user pause, and use cross-fade instead of jump when reduced. Trace: server reads cookie + CSS media query -> motion class -> carousel pause control -> reduced cross-fade -> SSR-safe no flash -> tests assert paused + no motion.

after_state.description: CSS-gated motion + server-read user toggle, pausable carousel, reduced-motion cross-fade, SSR-safe.

files.purpose: Disables transforms under OS reduced-motion or a user toggle, falling back to a gentle cross-fade.
Defaults to paused under reduced motion, exposes a pause/play control, and only announces slides when paused (no focus theft).
Reads the user motion toggle on the server to set data-motion before paint, avoiding a flash and hydration mismatch.

migration_strategy: Add motion.css gates + server toggle behind a `motionPrefs` flag
Ship CSS reduced-motion media query first (pure CSS, safe)
Enable the carousel pause control and reduced-motion default
Canary; verify no motion flash and SSR/CSR markup match
Ramp to 100%; expose the user toggle in settings
Remove the flag after the ADA complaint is resolved and 2.2.2 passes
testing_strategy: Playwright fixture with emulateMedia reduced-motion: load onboarding and assert the parallax element's computed transition is 'none' and the carousel starts paused (Pause button shows 'Play', aria-pressed true). Auto-advance test (motion on): assert the slide index advances after the interval and the live region is 'off' so SR isn't interrupted. Pause side-effect: click pause and assert the interval is cleared (index stops) and aria-live flips to 'polite'. SSR test: assert the server HTML already carries data-motion='off' for a motion=off cookie (no flash) and hydration logs no mismatch. Negative test: with reduced motion, slides cross-fade (opacity transition) rather than jumping.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading matchMedia only in a client effect produces a frame of full-motion parallax before hydration (a flash that can trigger vestibular symptoms) and a server/client markup mismatch, while the carousel still auto-advances with no pause, failing 2.2.2.

title: Localize Postgres-backed full-text search to be accent- and case-insensitive across locales
business_context: A logistics app stores addresses and runs Postgres full-text search with default English config, so a search for 'Munchen' never matches 'München' and Turkish dotless-i breaks. Operators in DE/TR couldn't find 18% of records by name, forcing manual lookups that added 40s per shipment.
constraints: Accent-insensitive matching (München == Munchen) via unaccent
Locale-correct stemming (German compound words, not English)
Case folding that respects Turkish i/İ rules where needed
Existing English search must not regress
Index must support 4M rows with <50ms p95 query latency

before_state.description: A single English tsvector column; no unaccent, English stemming applied to all languages.

issues: English config stems German/Turkish incorrectly
No unaccent, so München != Munchen
Single config can't serve multiple languages
Turkish case folding not handled

reasoning: Naive approach: lower() both sides and ILIKE. Rejected because ILIKE can't use the GIN FTS index at scale (seq scan on 4M rows) and still ignores accents/stemming. Rejected one global config (wrong stemming per language). Chosen path: install unaccent, build a per-row tsvector using a language column to choose the right config, wrap in an immutable unaccent function for index use, and query with the matching config. Trace: row language -> choose regconfig -> immutable unaccent in generated tsvector -> GIN index -> query negotiates same config -> tests assert München match + latency.

after_state.description: unaccent + per-language tsvector config + immutable wrapper, GIN-indexed for fast multilingual search.

files.purpose: Provides an IMMUTABLE unaccent so it can participate in a generated column and index, enabling accent-insensitive match.
Builds a per-row tsvector using the row's language config over unaccented text; GIN index keeps it fast.
Queries with the locale's text-search config and the same unaccent so accents/case fold and stemming match the index.

migration_strategy: Add unaccent + f_unaccent and a nullable lang column (default english) — non-breaking
Backfill lang from existing address country; rebuild tsv in batches concurrently
Build the new GIN index CONCURRENTLY to avoid table locks
Canary German/Turkish queries; verify München/Munchen parity and p95 latency
Switch the query path to config-aware search behind a flag, ramp to 100%
Drop the old English-only tsv/index after the new index serves all traffic
testing_strategy: SQL fixture seeds places with 'München' (lang german) and 'Istanbul' (lang turkish). Assert search('munchen','de') returns the München row (unaccent match) and search('istanbul','tr') matches with Turkish folding. Output assert: results ranked by ts_rank. Index side-effect: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a 4M-row table shows a Bitmap Index Scan on places_tsv_idx (not Seq Scan) and p95 < 50ms. Negative/regression test: an English query 'warehouse' still matches and ordering is unchanged. Chaos test: insert a row mid-backfill and assert the generated tsv populates immediately (generated column) without a missed index entry.
what_breaks_naive_approach: lower()+ILIKE can't use the GIN index, forcing a sequential scan over 4M rows (seconds, not ms), and a single 'english' FTS config stems German compounds and Turkish words wrong while never matching accented forms.

title: Add accessible accessible accordion semantics and keyboard support to the Vue FAQ component
business_context: A B2B SaaS help center FAQ is built with clickable divs that toggle a CSS class. Screen-reader users can't tell sections are expandable or which are open, and keyboard users can't operate them. Support deflection dropped because assistive-tech users couldn't self-serve, increasing tickets 14%.
constraints: Headers must be buttons with aria-expanded and aria-controls
Panels must be linked via id and hidden when collapsed
Arrow Up/Down move between headers; Home/End jump to first/last
Must support optional single-open (accordion) vs multi-open mode
No change to the existing slot-based content API

before_state.description: Div headers toggle a class; no roles, no aria, no keyboard.

issues: Header is a div: no button role, no aria-expanded
Panel not linked via aria-controls/id
No keyboard operation or arrow navigation
Collapsed panel uses v-show but isn't aria-hidden-correct

reasoning: Naive approach: add @keydown.enter to the div. Rejected because a div still announces nothing and Space won't work; arrow navigation between headers is also missing. Rejected a third-party accordion (breaks the slot API). Chosen path: render header as a <button> with aria-expanded/controls, link the panel by id, add a roving keyboard handler for Arrow/Home/End, and support single vs multi open. Trace: header button -> aria-expanded/controls -> panel id + hidden -> keyboard roving among headers -> mode prop -> tests assert expand/keyboard.

after_state.description: Button headers with aria-expanded/controls, linked panels, arrow roving, single/multi modes.

files.purpose: Uses h3>button headers with aria-expanded/controls, region panels linked by id, arrow/Home/End roving, and single vs multi open.

migration_strategy: Render the button/aria version behind a `faqA11y` flag; visuals identical
Enable on staging; run axe + NVDA/VoiceOver operate-and-navigate test
Verify single-open and multi-open modes via the prop
Canary on the help center; monitor AT-user self-serve deflection
Ramp to 100%; remove the div-based branch and flag
testing_strategy: Vue Test Utils fixture mounts Faq with 3 items; assert each header is a button with aria-expanded='false' and aria-controls pointing at a hidden region. Click header 1 and assert aria-expanded becomes 'true' and the panel's hidden attribute is removed. Keyboard test: focus header 0, dispatch ArrowDown and assert focus moves to header 1, End moves to the last. Mode side-effect: with single=true, open item 0 then item 1 and assert item 0 collapses (set cleared). Negative test: Space/Enter on a header toggles (native button) and the panel region is aria-labelledby its header.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding @keydown.enter to a div still leaves it announced as plain text (no expandable button), Space does nothing, and there's no arrow navigation between sections, so AT users can neither discover nor operate the FAQ.

title: Build a translation key extraction + missing-key CI gate for the React/TypeScript monorepo
business_context: A social app's React codebase has 4,200 t('...') calls but no extraction pipeline, so translators get an outdated key list and 12% of keys ship untranslated, rendering raw 'profile.bio.placeholder' to users. Missing keys are only caught in production, generating embarrassing screenshots on social media.
constraints: Statically extract all t('key') and useT keys at build time
Fail CI if a key used in code has no entry in the base (en) catalog
Detect orphaned keys (in catalog, unused in code) to prune
Support dynamic keys via an allowlist so they aren't false positives
Run in <30s on a 4,200-key, 900-file codebase

before_state.description: Keys are referenced ad hoc; the en catalog is hand-maintained and drifts from code.

issues: No tooling links code keys to the catalog
Missing keys (placeholder) only surface in production
Catalog accumulates orphaned keys nobody prunes
Translators work from a stale manual list

reasoning: Naive approach: grep for t( at runtime. Rejected because grep can't parse template/multiline calls reliably and dynamic keys cause false positives. Rejected a heavy i18next-parser config that doesn't fit the custom useT API. Chosen path: an AST-based extractor (ts-morph) that collects literal t() keys, diffs against the en catalog, fails CI on missing keys, reports orphans, and honors an allowlist for dynamic keys. Trace: source AST -> collect literal keys -> load en catalog -> diff missing/orphan -> allowlist filter -> CI exit code -> tests assert detection.

after_state.description: AST extractor + catalog diff + CI gate with dynamic-key allowlist and orphan reporting.

files.purpose: AST-based extractor that collects literal t() keys, diffs against en, fails CI on missing keys, and reports orphans, honoring a dynamic-key allowlist.

files.content:
[
  "status.*",
  "notification.kind.like",
  "notification.kind.follow"
]
files.purpose: Allowlist for keys constructed dynamically so they aren't flagged as missing or orphaned.

files.content:
name: i18n-keys
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  keys:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20 }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx tsx scripts/extract-keys.ts
files.purpose: CI gate that runs the extractor on every PR and blocks merge when keys are missing from en.

migration_strategy: Land the extractor in report-only mode (warn, exit 0) to baseline missing keys
Backfill the existing missing keys into en.json with the team
Flip the gate to fail (exit 1) once en is complete
Add the dynamic-keys allowlist for legitimately-constructed keys
Wire a prune step that removes orphaned keys after translator sign-off
Extend the diff to non-en catalogs to track translation coverage %
testing_strategy: Vitest fixture creates an in-memory project with a file calling t('a.b') and t('x.y') and an en.json missing 'x.y'; assert extractKeys returns {'a.b','x.y'} and the missing diff is ['x.y']. CI side-effect: run the script and assert process exit code is 1 when a key is missing and 0 when complete. Allowlist test: add 'x.y' (or a glob) to dynamic-keys.json and assert it's no longer reported missing. Orphan test: add an unused 'z.q' to en.json and assert it's reported as orphaned. Performance test: run against a synthetic 900-file/4,200-key tree and assert completion under 30s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Runtime grep for t( misses multiline/template calls and flags every dynamically-built key as a false positive, so it can't be a reliable merge gate and missing literal keys still leak to production.

title: Make the React video player captions, transcript, and controls fully accessible (WCAG 1.2.2/2.1)
business_context: An ed-tech platform's custom React video player has no captions toggle, no keyboard control, and a non-labeled progress slider. Deaf students can't follow lectures (1.2.2 Captions fails) and motor-impaired students can't use the player by keyboard. A university client made remediation a contract condition.
constraints: Captions toggle with multiple caption tracks (multilingual)
All controls keyboard-operable with visible focus
Progress slider must be an accessible slider with aria-valuenow/min/max
Time announced to SR without spamming on every frame
Must work with the existing HLS source pipeline unchanged

before_state.description: Custom controls are unlabeled divs; no caption track UI; the seek bar is a styled div.

issues: Controls are divs with no role/label or keyboard
No captions/subtitles UI or <track>
Seek bar is a div, not an accessible slider
No time/state announcements for SR

reasoning: Naive approach: add tabindex to the control divs. Rejected because a div seek bar can't expose slider semantics and still isn't operable by arrow keys. Rejected swapping to the bare native controls (loses brand styling and multi-track UX). Chosen path: real <button> controls with labels, a native-backed <input type=range> styled as the seek slider with aria value attributes, <track> elements with a captions menu, and a throttled polite time announcement. Trace: HLS source unchanged -> button controls -> range slider with aria-valuenow -> track menu toggles textTracks -> throttled live time -> tests assert keyboard + captions.

after_state.description: Labeled button controls, accessible range seek slider, caption track menu, throttled time announcements.

files.purpose: Real button play/pause, a native range seek slider with aria value text, caption tracks, and throttled (every 30s) time announcements.
Accessible captions menu (menuitemradio) toggling the video's textTracks mode for multilingual subtitles.

migration_strategy: Add <track> + accessible controls behind a `playerA11y` flag; HLS source untouched
Enable for one course; verify keyboard play/seek and caption toggle with NVDA
Add caption tracks for the top 3 lecture languages
Canary; confirm seek slider exposes aria-valuetext and time announces every 30s
Ramp to 100%; remove the div-control branch and flag
Audit re-test for 1.2.2 and 2.1.1 before contract sign-off
testing_strategy: Testing Library + jsdom video mock fixture: render Player with two tracks; assert the play control is a button with aria-label 'Play' toggling to 'Pause' and aria-pressed updates. Seek test: the range input has min=0, max=duration, and setting its value sets video.currentTime (side-effect on the element). Caption test: open the CC menu (aria-expanded true), choose 'Español' and assert that track's textTrack.mode becomes 'showing' and others 'disabled'. Announcement test: advance currentTime by 30s and assert the polite region reads '0:30', but a 5s advance does NOT announce (throttle). Negative test: keyboard-only — Tab to the slider and ArrowRight increments currentTime via native range behavior.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding tabindex to control divs gives focus but no operable semantics: a div seek bar can't be arrow-keyed or expose aria-valuenow, and without <track> elements deaf students get no captions at all, failing 1.2.2.

title: Localize CSV/Excel export headers and number formats by user locale in the Node reporting service
business_context: A fintech reporting service exports transaction CSVs with English headers and US number/date formats. German finance teams importing into Excel get '#NAME?' errors because '1,234.56' is parsed as text, and column headers like 'Amount' aren't understood. Manual reformatting wastes ~3 hours per monthly close across 60 client teams.
constraints: Headers localized per requested locale
Numbers/dates formatted so Excel in that locale parses them natively
CSV delimiter must follow the locale (';' for de-DE Excel)
UTF-8 BOM so Excel detects encoding for non-ASCII headers
Stream-generate exports up to 2M rows without buffering all in memory

before_state.description: CSV uses English headers, ',' delimiter, US number format, and no BOM.

issues: English headers only
',' delimiter conflicts with de-DE decimal comma
US number/date format mis-parsed by localized Excel
No BOM; non-ASCII headers garble; whole file buffered in memory

reasoning: Naive approach: swap '.' for ',' for German. Rejected because that collides with the ',' field delimiter and breaks CSV parsing; also ignores headers, dates, and BOM. Rejected generating .xlsx (heavier, and clients want CSV). Chosen path: locale-aware delimiter (';' when decimal is ','), Intl-formatted numbers/dates, localized header catalog, a UTF-8 BOM, and a streaming generator for large exports. Trace: locale -> separators decide delimiter -> Intl formats values -> header catalog -> BOM prefix -> stream rows -> tests assert de-DE Excel-parseable output.

after_state.description: Locale-aware delimiter + Intl formatting + localized headers + BOM, streamed for 2M rows.

files.purpose: Derives a safe delimiter from the locale's decimal symbol and formats numbers/dates so localized Excel parses them.
Streams a BOM-prefixed, locale-delimited CSV with localized headers and Intl-formatted cells for 2M rows without buffering.
Negotiates locale and pipes the streamed CSV to the response, keeping memory flat for large exports.

migration_strategy: Add locale-aware export behind an `i18nExport` flag; default en/',' unchanged
Enable de-DE for one client; verify Excel imports without #NAME? errors
Confirm BOM presence and ';' delimiter open cleanly in German Excel
Canary all de/fr clients; measure manual-reformatting time drop
Ramp to all locales; switch the streaming path on for large exports
Remove the buffered toCsv after streaming serves all traffic
testing_strategy: Vitest fixture builds a CSV for de-DE from rows [{date:'2026-01-31',amountCents:123456,desc:'Miete'}]; assert the stream starts with the BOM '\uFEFF', header 'Datum;Betrag;Beschreibung', and the row '31.01.26;1234,56;Miete' (';' delimiter, comma decimal, dd.MM.yy). Delimiter side-effect: a desc containing ';' is quoted. en-US asserts ',' delimiter and '1234.56'. Memory/streaming test: generate 2,000,000 rows via an async generator and assert peak RSS stays bounded (no full-buffer) and the stream ends. Negative test: a missing accept-language defaults to en and produces a comma-delimited file Excel-US can open.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Swapping '.' for ',' in German output collides with the ',' field delimiter, so Excel splits the decimal into two columns and corrupts every row; without a BOM and localized delimiter, non-ASCII headers garble and numbers import as text (#NAME?).

title: Add accessible data-table semantics (caption, scope, sortable, row-headers) to the NestJS-backed React grid
business_context: A healthcare lab-results table in React renders a grid of <div>s for CSS control. Screen-reader users can't associate a value with its column/row, so a clinician using JAWS read a potassium value under the wrong patient. The org classified it as a safety risk and mandated real table semantics.
constraints: Use <table> with caption, <th scope>, and row headers
Sortable columns must expose aria-sort and keyboard activation
Virtualized rows (10k) must keep header association correct
Numeric cells must be SR-readable with units, not bare numbers
No change to the NestJS results API contract

before_state.description: A div-grid with CSS grid layout; no table semantics, so SR can't map cells to headers.

issues: No table/th/scope so cells aren't associated with headers
No caption describing the table
No row header to anchor each result to a patient/test
Numeric cells lack units for SR

reasoning: Naive approach: add role=grid/gridcell to the divs. Rejected because ARIA grid semantics are for interactive widgets and don't give SR the simple column/row association of a real data table; getting scope right with role=grid is error-prone. Rejected keeping divs with aria-describedby links (fragile at 10k rows). Chosen path: render a semantic <table> with <caption>, column <th scope=col>, a row <th scope=row> per result, aria-sort on sortable columns, and units in cell text; virtualize while keeping <thead> persistent. Trace: data -> table head with scope=col -> row header scope=row -> aria-sort -> unit-augmented cell -> virtualization keeps thead -> tests assert SR association.

after_state.description: Semantic table with caption, scoped headers, row headers, aria-sort, units, and persistent header virtualization.

files.purpose: Real table with caption, scope=col headers, a scope=row header per result, aria-sort, and unit-bearing numeric cells.
Windows 10k rows while each rendered slice is a complete semantic table (thead re-rendered) so scope associations always hold.

migration_strategy: Render the semantic table behind a `tableSemantics` flag; CSS keeps the look
Enable on staging; run axe table-checks + JAWS cell-navigation review
Verify aria-sort and row-header association on a real lab dataset
Canary; confirm virtualization keeps headers associated while scrolling
Ramp to 100%; remove the div-grid branch and flag
Re-audit the safety finding before clinical sign-off
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders ResultsTable; assert getByRole('table') has an accessible name from <caption>, column headers expose role=columnheader with scope, and each row has a rowheader. Sort test: click a sortable header and assert its th aria-sort toggles ascending/descending while others read 'none'. Units side-effect: assert a potassium cell renders '4.1 mmol/L' (SR-readable). Virtualization test: scroll a 10k-row VirtualTable and assert the rendered slice still contains a thead so cell-to-header mapping holds. Negative test: a non-sortable column header has no aria-sort attribute and no button.
what_breaks_naive_approach: role=grid/gridcell on divs models an interactive widget, not a data table, so JAWS doesn't announce 'column: Potassium, row: Patient A' associations, and a clinician can read a value under the wrong header — the exact safety bug.

title: Implement locale-aware sorting (collation) for name lists in the Postgres-backed Go API
business_context: A B2B SaaS contacts directory sorts names with default byte-order collation, so Swedish 'Ä' sorts after 'Z' wrongly, German 'ö' is misplaced, and 'é' sorts after 'z'. Users in Nordic and German markets complained that alphabetical browse is broken, and one enterprise rejected the product for 'unusable' contact lists.
constraints: Sort must follow the user's locale collation rules (sv, de, en)
Must use an index so sorting 500k contacts stays <80ms p95
Locale chosen per request, not a global DB setting
Backward-compatible: en sorting must not change
Handle ties deterministically (secondary sort by id)

before_state.description: ORDER BY name uses the database's default (often C/byte) collation, mis-ordering accented letters.

issues: Uses default collation; accented letters misordered
Same order for every locale (sv/de need different rules)
No deterministic tiebreak
No index aligned with the sort, risking slow sorts

reasoning: Naive approach: lower() and strip accents before sorting. Rejected because that destroys locale rules (sv treats Ä as a distinct letter after Z, not as A) and loses an index. Rejected setting a global DB collation (can't vary per request). Chosen path: use ICU collations (Postgres COLLATE with locale-specific provider), pass a validated collation per request, add per-collation expression indexes for the hot locales, and add id as a secondary sort. Trace: request locale -> map to ICU collation -> ORDER BY name COLLATE -> matching expression index -> id tiebreak -> tests assert sv/de order + latency.

after_state.description: Per-request ICU collation in ORDER BY with matching indexes and deterministic tiebreak.

files.purpose: Defines ICU collations per locale and expression indexes that match the ORDER BY so sorts use the index.
Selects a whitelisted collation per request and orders by it with an id tiebreak, leveraging the matching index.

migration_strategy: Create ICU collations + indexes CONCURRENTLY (non-blocking, additive)
Default locale to en_icu so existing en ordering is preserved
Canary sv/de requests; verify Ä/ö/é ordering against native-speaker expectations
Confirm EXPLAIN shows index scans, p95 < 80ms on 500k rows
Ramp to all locales; only whitelisted collation names are interpolated
Drop legacy default-collation queries after telemetry confirms index usage
testing_strategy: Go integration test seeds names ['Zorn','Ärlig','Bok'] and asserts List(db,'sv',10) returns ['Bok','Zorn','Ärlig'] (Swedish Ä after Z) while List(db,'de',10) returns ['Ärlig','Bok','Zorn'] (German ä near a). Tiebreak side-effect: two identical names sort by ascending id deterministically across runs. Index assert: EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows an Index Scan on contacts_name_sv (not a Sort node) and latency under 80ms on a 500k-row fixture. Negative/security test: an unknown locale falls back to en_icu and the collation name is never taken from raw user input (whitelist), preventing SQL injection.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Stripping accents and lowercasing breaks Swedish/German alphabetical rules (Ä is a separate final letter in sv, not 'a') and abandons the index, turning a 500k-row browse into a full sort that blows the latency budget.

title: Add accessible modal-free inline date picker with full keyboard grid to the React booking flow
business_context: A travel booking site's React calendar is a grid of clickable spans; keyboard users can't navigate days, screen readers announce nothing useful, and disabled dates aren't conveyed. Conversion analysis shows keyboard users complete bookings at one-third the rate of mouse users. WCAG 2.1.1 and 4.1.2 failed.
constraints: Day grid must be navigable with arrow keys (and PageUp/Down for months)
Selected/disabled/today states must be in the a11y tree (aria-selected, aria-disabled)
Grid must use a roving tabindex (one tab stop)
Localized month/day names and first-day-of-week per locale
Announce the focused date and selection to SR

before_state.description: Day cells are spans with onClick; no roles, no keyboard, no state semantics.

issues: Day cells are spans: no role/keyboard
No roving tabindex; can't arrow-navigate
Selected/disabled/today not exposed to SR
Month/day names not localized; week starts Sunday always

reasoning: Naive approach: tabindex=0 on every day. Rejected because that makes 30+ tab stops and still no arrow navigation. Rejected a date-picker library swap mid-flow. title: Add bidirectional (bidi) text isolation to prevent mixed RTL/LTR username corruption in the React chat
business_context: A social chat app concatenates usernames and messages; when an Arabic username appears next to LTR content (URLs, @mentions, numbers), the bidi algorithm reorders characters so 'user@123' renders scrambled and a trailing punctuation jumps to the wrong side. Users reported messages that 'look hacked', and a phishing vector emerged where RTL override chars hid malicious URLs.
constraints: Isolate user-controlled spans so their direction can't leak into surrounding text
Strip dangerous bidi control characters (RLO/LRO override) from user input
Auto-detect first strong character to set per-span direction
Must not change rendering of pure-LTR or pure-RTL messages
Works in server-rendered HTML and client updates identically

before_state.description: Username and message are concatenated with no bidi isolation; user input can contain override controls.

issues: No bidi isolation; RTL username reorders the following ': message'
User input may contain RLO/LRO override chars (spoofing)
Direction not auto-detected per span
Mixed numbers/URLs in RTL context render scrambled

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap everything in dir="ltr". Rejected because it forces Arabic messages to render LTR (wrong) and still lets override chars spoof. Rejected hardcoding dir per message language (unknown for mixed content). Chosen path: sanitize bidi control characters from user input, wrap each user-controlled span with Unicode isolates (FSI...PDI) via the bdi element / dir=auto, letting the browser auto-detect direction per isolated span. Trace: input -> strip RLO/LRO/etc -> render in <bdi dir=auto> -> isolate boundaries stop direction leak -> same on SSR/CSR -> tests assert isolation + sanitization.

after_state.description: Bidi control sanitization + <bdi dir=auto> isolation so each span's direction is detected and contained.

files.purpose: Removes RLO/LRO/PDF and isolate/override controls from user input to block bidi-spoofing attacks.
Wraps each user-controlled value in <bdi dir=auto> (Unicode isolation) after sanitizing controls, so direction is auto-detected and contained.
Sanitizes bidi controls at ingest so persisted messages are safe regardless of the rendering layer.

migration_strategy: Add stripBidiControls at ingest behind a `bidiSafe` flag (server-side, safe)

Backfill: sanitize stored messages in a batch migration

testing_strategy: Vitest fixture: render ChatLine with user 'عمر' and text 'check @bob https://x.io 123'; assert the user and message are each wrapped in a <bdi dir=auto> so the separator ': ' and the LTR URL/number render in logical order (snapshot stable across SSR and CSR). Security side-effect: sanitizeMessage strips a U+202E RLO override from text and stores the cleaned string; assert the stored length excludes the control char. Negative test: a pure-English message renders identically to before (no isolate-induced layout change). Phishing test: an input crafted with RLO to disguise 'gpj.exe' as 'exe.jpg' has the control stripped so the real extension is shown.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping the whole line in dir=ltr mis-renders Arabic messages and still permits RLO/LRO override characters to reorder text for spoofing; without per-span isolation an RTL username bleeds its direction into the following ': message' and trailing punctuation.

business_context: A B2B SaaS design system removed all focus outlines with *:focus { outline: none } for aesthetics. Forced-colors test: emulate forced-colors active and assert an outline (system Highlight) replaces the box-shadow. title: Localize push notification copy and respect quiet hours per recipient timezone in the FastAPI worker
business_context: A fintech app's FastAPI push worker sends English alerts at fixed UTC times, so customers in India get 'Payment due' notifications at 3am local and in English. Complaints about night-time pings drove a 6% uninstall spike in APAC, and non-English speakers ignored alerts they couldn't read.
constraints: Localize copy to recipient locale with ICU plurals
Respect quiet hours (22:00-08:00) in the recipient's IANA timezone
Deferred sends must reschedule to the next allowed window, not drop
Idempotent across worker retries (no duplicate pushes)
Throughput 3k pushes/sec across 6 workers; per-message budget < 1ms compute

before_state.description: Worker sends English copy at a UTC cron time with no timezone or quiet-hours awareness.

files.content:
from datetime import datetime, timezone

async def send_due_reminders(users):
    for u in users:
        body = f"Payment of {u.amount} is due"
        await push(u.device_token, 'Reminder', body)
issues: English-only copy
No quiet-hours check; pings at any local hour
Amount not localized; no plural handling
No idempotency across retries

reasoning: Naive approach: add a UTC hour check. Rejected because quiet hours are local and a single UTC window pings the wrong hours globally. Rejected dropping notifications outside the window (users miss them). Chosen path: localize copy via ICU per recipient, compute local time with zoneinfo, and if inside quiet hours reschedule to the next 08:00 local instead of dropping, guarded by a Redis idempotency key. Trace: user locale+tz -> ICU render -> local-time quiet-hours check -> reschedule or send -> Redis idempotency -> tests assert reschedule + locale.

after_state.description: Locale ICU copy + zoneinfo quiet-hours with reschedule + Redis idempotency.

files.purpose: Renders localized, ICU-formatted push copy with an en fallback, compiled-and-cached for throughput.

files.content:
from datetime import datetime, time
from zoneinfo import ZoneInfo

QUIET_START, QUIET_END = time(22, 0), time(8, 0)

def next_allowed(now_utc: datetime, tz_name: str) -> datetime | None:
    tz = ZoneInfo(tz_name or 'UTC')
    local = now_utc.astimezone(tz)
    t = local.time()
    in_quiet = t >= QUIET_START or t < QUIET_END
    if not in_quiet:
        return None  # send now
    # schedule for 08:00 local today or tomorrow
    target = local.replace(hour=8, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
    if t >= QUIET_START:
        target = target.replace(day=local.day) + (target - target)  # next morning
        from datetime import timedelta
        target = (local + timedelta(days=1)).replace(hour=8, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
    return target.astimezone(ZoneInfo('UTC'))
files.purpose: Computes whether 'now' is in the recipient's local quiet hours and the next 08:00-local UTC send time.

files.content:
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from .i18n import render
from .quiet import next_allowed

r = aioredis.Redis()

async def send_due_reminders(users, schedule):
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    for u in users:
        idem = f"push:{u.notif_id}"
        if not await r.set(idem, '1', ex=86400, nx=True):
            continue  # already handled (retry-safe)
        when = next_allowed(now, u.tz)
        body = render(u.locale or 'en', 'push.due', amount=u.amount, count=u.count)
        if when is None:
            await push(u.device_token, render(u.locale or 'en', 'push.title'), body)
        else:
            await r.delete(idem)  # allow the rescheduled job to send later
            await schedule(u.notif_id, when)
files.purpose: Localizes copy, defers to the next allowed local window instead of dropping, and uses Redis SETNX for retry idempotency.

migration_strategy: Add locale rendering + quiet-hours behind a `quietHours` flag (default off)
Enable localization first; copy localized but timing unchanged
Turn on quiet-hours reschedule for APAC timezones; verify no 3am sends
Canary; confirm deferred notifications arrive at ~08:00 local, not dropped
Ramp to all regions; keep idempotency keys on permanently
Remove the flag after the APAC uninstall rate normalizes
testing_strategy: Pytest fixture: with a recipient tz 'Asia/Kolkata' and now=2026-06-21T18:00:00Z (23:30 IST), assert next_allowed returns the next 08:00 IST as a UTC datetime, so send is deferred; with now during IST daytime, next_allowed returns None (send immediately). i18n side-effect: render('hi','push.due',...) returns Hindi copy with the correct plural. Idempotency test: process the same notif_id twice and assert push is called at most once and SETNX returns False on the retry. Negative/chaos test: a missing tz defaults to UTC without raising; simulate a worker retry storm of 1000 messages and assert zero duplicate pushes and that deferred jobs are scheduled exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single UTC hour gate pings APAC users at 3am because quiet hours are local, and dropping out-of-window notifications means users never see payment reminders at all.

title: Store and serve user-generated content in multiple locales with a MongoDB translation model and fallback chain
business_context: A B2B SaaS knowledge base stores articles as a single 'body' string in MongoDB. constraints: Per-locale fields with a fallback chain (exact > base lang > default)

before_state.description: A single body field holds one language; no locale dimension or fallback.

issues: Single body string; no locale dimension
No fallback chain
Editors overwrite one field with multiple languages
Search can't index per-locale

reasoning: Naive approach: add bodyDe, bodyFr columns. Rejected because it doesn't scale to N locales or express a fallback chain, and breaks adding a locale without a schema change. Rejected a separate translations collection requiring a join per read (latency). Chosen path: an embedded map of locale->{title,body,status}, a fallback resolver (exact > base > default), a backward-compatible virtual 'body' for old clients, and per-locale text indexes. Trace: locale request -> resolve via chain -> embedded translations map -> virtual body for legacy -> per-locale text index -> migration wraps old body into en -> tests assert fallback.

after_state.description: Embedded locale map with fallback resolver, legacy-compatible virtual, and per-locale search indexing.

files.purpose: Embedded locale map plus a fallback resolver (exact>base>default>en) and per-locale text indexes.
Returns a legacy-compatible {body} shape resolved via fallback and exposes per-locale translation status for editors.
Moves existing single-language content into translations.en without data loss, reversible by reading translations.en back.

Run the migration wrapping existing body into translations.en (published)
Serve via resolve() behind an `i18nArticles` flag; legacy body still works
