Drop the legacy body field after all reads use resolve()
testing_strategy: Vitest fixture seeds an article with translations { en:{published}, de:{published} }; assert getArticle(slug,'de-AT') resolves to de (base-lang fallback) with resolvedLocale 'de', and getArticle(slug,'fr') falls back to en. Draft test: a de draft is skipped and falls back to en (only published served). Migration side-effect: run up() on a legacy doc and assert translations.en.body equals the old body and the top-level body is unset (no loss, reversible). Search index assert: a text search on translations.de.body uses idx_text_de. Negative test: an article with no published locales returns null rather than serving a draft. Backward-compat: the returned object still has a top-level body for old clients.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding bodyDe/bodyFr columns can't express a fallback chain (de-AT->de->en) and forces a schema migration for every new locale, while editors keep overwriting a single field; a separate join collection adds a per-read lookup that hurts latency.

No browser-back sync

reasoning: Naive approach: focus the heading on every render. Rejected because that steals focus on unrelated re-renders and on initial load. Rejected announcing via the heading alone (SR may not read it on swap). Chosen path: move focus to the step heading only when the step index changes, announce 'Step N of M' via a polite live region, focus the first invalid field on validation failure, and sync step with history state. Trace: step change -> focus heading + announce -> validation -> focus first invalid -> popstate sync -> tests assert focus + announcement.

after_state.description: Step-change focus to heading, 'Step N of M' announcement, invalid-field focus, history sync.

files.purpose: Moves focus to the step heading only on step change, announces 'Step N of M', focuses first invalid field on validation, and syncs with history.

migration_strategy: Add focus/announcement logic behind a `wizardA11y` flag
Enable on staging; verify NVDA announces step changes and focus lands on heading
Wire validation to focus the first invalid field on a failing Next
Canary; compare AT-user step-2 drop-off to baseline
Ramp to 100%; verify browser-back syncs step and focus
Remove the flag after 2.4.3/4.1.3 pass
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders a 4-step wizard; click Next and assert document.activeElement is the step-2 heading and the polite region reads 'Step 2 of 4: ...'. Initial-load guard: assert focus is NOT stolen to the heading on first render (firstRender ref). Validation test: make onValidate return an error and render a field with aria-invalid='true'; clicking Next focuses that field and does NOT advance. History side-effect: after advancing, assert history.replaceState was called with { step }, and dispatching a popstate with state.step=0 returns to step 1. Negative test: clicking Back on step 1 does nothing (disabled) and doesn't announce.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Focusing the heading on every render steals focus during unrelated state updates and on initial load; without that, focus stays on the old Next button (now disabled/moved), stranding SR users who also never hear that the step changed.

title: Localize SMS/OTP message templates and handle GSM-7 vs UCS-2 encoding length limits across locales
business_context: A fintech OTP service (Node) sends English SMS via a single template. Localizing to Arabic/Greek/Cyrillic silently switched messages to UCS-2 encoding, halving the 160->70 char limit, so localized messages split into 2-3 segments (tripling cost) or got truncated, cutting off the OTP code. Delivery failures spiked in MENA.
constraints: Localized templates per locale with the OTP code never truncated
Detect GSM-7 vs UCS-2 and respect segment length (160 vs 70, multipart 153/67)
Warn/error at build time if a template+args can exceed one segment for the code
Keep sender + code placement consistent for client parsing (autofill)
Cost guardrail: reject templates that would exceed N segments

before_state.description: Single English template; no encoding awareness, so localized text overflows segments and truncates the code.

issues: English-only
No encoding detection; UCS-2 locales overflow the 70-char limit
Long messages truncate the OTP code
No segment/cost guardrail

reasoning: Naive approach: translate the template and send. Rejected because non-GSM-7 characters force UCS-2 (70-char segments) and a translated string easily overflows, truncating the code. Rejected always using UCS-2 (doubles cost for Latin locales). Chosen path: per-locale templates, GSM-7 detection to choose the segment budget, place the code early so it survives, and a build-time guard rejecting templates that exceed the segment budget. Trace: locale -> template -> encoding detection -> segment math -> code-first ordering -> build guard -> tests assert no truncation + segment count.

after_state.description: Per-locale templates with GSM-7/UCS-2 detection, code-first ordering, and a build-time segment guard.

files.purpose: Detects GSM-7 vs UCS-2 and computes SMS segment count using the correct single/multipart limits.
Per-locale templates with code placed first so it survives, plus a build-time guard rejecting over-budget templates.
Sends with the detected encoding and a runtime cost guardrail rejecting excessive segments.

migration_strategy: Add encoding detection + per-locale templates behind an `i18nOtp` flag
Run assertSingleSegment in CI so over-budget templates fail the build
Enable ar/el/ru; verify single-segment delivery and intact code
Canary MENA traffic; monitor delivery-failure and segment-count metrics
Ramp to all locales; keep the runtime guardrail permanently
Tune templates per locale to fit one UCS-2 segment where possible
testing_strategy: Vitest fixture: segments('123456 is your code') returns {encoding:'GSM-7', count:1}; segments(Arabic template) returns {encoding:'UCS-2', count:1} only if under 70 chars. assertSingleSegment throws for a deliberately long Arabic template (build guard side-effect). Code-integrity test: for every locale template, assert the OTP code appears in the first segment's first 20 chars so truncation can't cut it. Send test: sendOtp routes encoding 'UCS-2' for Arabic to the provider (assert the option passed). Negative/cost test: a 3-segment message causes sendOtp to throw rather than send. Autofill test: assert the code is positioned for the client's OTP-autofill regex consistently across locales.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Translating the template without encoding awareness silently switches Arabic/Greek/Cyrillic to UCS-2 (70-char segments), so the longer localized text overflows and truncates the verification code, breaking authentication and tripling SMS cost.

title: Build a screen-reader-friendly virtualized list that keeps set-size and position info correct
business_context: A social app's virtualized feed (React + react-window) renders only visible items, so screen readers announce 'list item' with no position and report a tiny set size (only mounted rows), making it impossible to know there are 10,000 posts or where you are. AT users described the feed as 'a black hole'.
constraints: aria-setsize/aria-posinset must reflect the FULL list, not the rendered window
Container must be role=feed or list with correct semantics
Keyboard navigation must load/scroll to off-screen items
Focused item must stay mounted and announced when scrolled into view
Must keep react-window's performance (no rendering all 10k)

before_state.description: Virtualized rows have no setsize/posinset; SR sees only the mounted subset.

issues: No role on the list container or items
No aria-setsize/aria-posinset (SR sees only mounted rows)
Keyboard can't reach off-screen items
Focused item gets unmounted on scroll, losing focus

reasoning: Naive approach: add role=list and let it be. Rejected because the browser computes set size from mounted children, so SR still reports only the window. Rejected rendering all 10k (defeats virtualization). Chosen path: role=feed with each item role=article carrying aria-setsize={total} and aria-posinset={index+1}, an aria-label on the container with the total, and keyboard handlers that scrollToItem and keep the focused index mounted via react-window's overscan. Trace: total count -> explicit setsize/posinset per item -> role=feed container -> keyboard scrollToItem -> overscan keeps focus mounted -> tests assert posinset.

after_state.description: role=feed with explicit aria-setsize/posinset, keyboard scroll-to-item, and focus-preserving overscan.

files.purpose: role=feed container with full-count label, each article exposing aria-setsize/posinset, keyboard scroll-to-item, and overscan to keep the focused row mounted.

migration_strategy: Add role=feed + setsize/posinset behind a `feedA11y` flag
Enable on staging; verify NVDA announces 'article 42 of 10000'
Wire keyboard navigation to scrollToItem with overscan
Canary; confirm focus survives scrolling (focused item stays mounted)
Ramp to 100%; keep react-window performance budget
Remove the flag after AT users confirm position/size announcements
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders a 10,000-item Feed (windowed). Assert the role=feed container's accessible name includes '10000' and each rendered article exposes aria-setsize='10000' and a 1-based aria-posinset matching its index, even though only ~8 are mounted. Keyboard test: ArrowDown updates the focused index and calls scrollToItem; pressing End sets focused to 9999 and the focused article mounts (overscan) and receives DOM focus. Performance side-effect: assert the number of mounted articles stays small (window + overscan), not 10000. Negative test: ArrowUp at index 0 stays at 0. SR assert: a row scrolled into focus has role=article and the correct posinset.
what_breaks_naive_approach: role=list alone lets the browser derive set size from mounted children, so SR reports only the ~8 windowed rows as the whole list; rendering all 10k to fix it would destroy the virtualization performance the feature depends on.

title: Localize and accessibility-label the chart axes/legends in the React analytics dashboard
business_context: A B2B SaaS analytics dashboard renders SVG charts with English axis labels, US number formats on ticks, and no text alternative, so screen-reader users get nothing and non-US users see '$1,234.5' on EUR revenue. An enterprise a11y review flagged charts as 'completely inaccessible' and localization flagged the tick formats.
constraints: Axis tick labels formatted by locale (numbers, currency, dates)
Chart must expose an accessible summary/table alternative for SR
Legend items keyboard-focusable with accessible names
Color-coded series must also be distinguishable non-visually (in the table)
No change to the existing chart data API

before_state.description: SVG chart with hardcoded English/US tick labels and no SR alternative.

issues: Axis label English-only, '$' hardcoded
Tick numbers not localized
No SR alternative (chart is invisible to AT)
Legend/series not keyboard-accessible or distinguishable non-visually

reasoning: Naive approach: add aria-label to the <svg>. Rejected because a single label can't convey trends/values; SR users need the data. Rejected rendering a hidden image description (stale, manual). Chosen path: format ticks via Intl per locale+currency, add role=img with a concise localized summary, and provide a visually-hidden data table alternative that conveys series values (also solving color-only). Trace: data -> Intl-formatted ticks -> role=img summary -> sr-only data table -> focusable legend -> tests assert table + formats.

after_state.description: Locale-formatted ticks, role=img summary, and a visually-hidden accessible data-table alternative.

files.purpose: Locale/currency-aware tick formatting for axis labels.
Adds a localized role=img summary and a visually-hidden data table conveying the underlying values for SR.
Visually-hidden semantic table giving SR users the exact data behind the chart, independent of color.

migration_strategy: Add Intl tick formatting + role=img summary behind a `chartA11y` flag
Add the visually-hidden DataTable alternative; verify with NVDA
Localize tick formats for de/fr/ja; verify EUR/JPY rendering
Canary; confirm SR users can read the data table for each chart
Ramp to 100%; extend the pattern to other chart types
Re-audit the 'inaccessible charts' finding
testing_strategy: Testing Library fixture renders RevenueChart for de-DE/EUR; assert the svg has role=img with a localized aria-label containing a compactly formatted EUR total ('1,2\u00a0Mio.\u00a0\u20ac'), and a visually-hidden table exists with a caption, scoped column headers, and a row per data point whose currency cells are EUR-formatted. i18n side-effect: switch to ja-JP/JPY and assert ticks render '¥' compact. Color-independence assert: the table conveys each series value as text (no reliance on color). Negative test: an empty points array renders an accessible empty-state summary, not a broken table. SR table test: getByRole('table') has an accessible name from caption.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single aria-label on the SVG can't convey the actual values or trends, so SR users still can't read the chart; and hardcoded '$' on EUR revenue with US tick formatting misleads non-US users about real figures.
